summary: Another wild day in Atlanta as a major development finally breaks ground in Reynoldstown near the BeltLine, while a new food hall from the Gansevoort Market founder gets ready to open in East Lake. Meanwhile, airport chaos continues with ICE agents deployed to Hartsfield-Jackson as TSA workers call out amid the federal shutdown.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown Corner Development Finally Breaks Ground Near BeltLine
topStory.summary: After sitting as empty lots for nearly five years, construction has started on 85 residential units at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace. Embry Development took over the project from Toll Brothers and is bringing townhomes and condos just a block from the Eastside Trail. This is exactly the kind of infill development Reynoldstown needs as the neighborhood continues its transformation.
topStory.body:
You know that corner where Chester meets Fulton Terrace in Reynoldstown? The one that's been sitting empty since they cleared the old buildings back in 2021? Well, finally there's some action happening. Embry Development Company has started construction on what they're calling Camber Crossing, bringing 85 new residential units to a prime spot just a block west of the BeltLine's Eastside Trail.

This project has had quite the journey. Toll Brothers originally permitted the development but ended up selling to Atlanta-based Embry Development two years ago. The new owners are taking a different approach with a mix of townhomes and for-sale condos, which honestly feels more appropriate for the neighborhood than whatever massive complex Toll Brothers might have built. At 2 acres, it's a decent-sized infill project that should help bridge the gap between Memorial Drive and the BeltLine trail.

For Reynoldstown residents, this represents both the promise and the pressure of living next to the BeltLine. On one hand, you're getting new housing options and increased walkability. On the other hand, well, you know how this story goes in every BeltLine neighborhood. But at least Embry seems committed to building something that fits the scale of the area rather than just maximizing units.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction activity at Chester Avenue development site in Reynoldstown

stories.headline: Decatur's Missing Middle Housing Village Gets Ready to Launch
stories.summary: Cottages at Midway is wrapping up construction on 18 standalone houses designed around shared courtyards and greenspace. The goal is creating more accessible single-family housing near downtown Decatur, where median home values have climbed above $700,000. It's the kind of creative infill project that could be a model for other ITP neighborhoods struggling with affordability.
stories.body:
Finally, someone's doing something creative about the housing crunch in Decatur. Cottages at Midway is putting the finishing touches on 18 standalone houses on Midway Road that are designed to feel more like a village than a typical subdivision. We're talking shared courtyards, greenspace, and houses that actually look like they belong in the neighborhood rather than some cookie-cutter development.

The whole concept is about creating 'missing middle' housing that falls between apartments and expensive single-family homes. With median home prices in Decatur pushing past $700,000, there's clearly demand for something more accessible. The developers are calling it a 'pocket neighborhood' and honestly, that feels about right. It's the kind of thoughtful density that preserves the character people love about Decatur while acknowledging that not everyone can afford a $750,000 house.

What I like about this project is that it's happening on underused lots rather than tearing down existing housing. The 2.1-acre site was basically vacant land, so this is pure addition to the housing stock. Plus, being right on Midway Road puts residents within walking distance of downtown Decatur and all those great restaurants and shops. If this model works, hopefully we'll see more creative infill like this throughout the metro.
stories.imageAlt: Site map showing layout of Cottages at Midway development in Decatur

stories.headline: West End BeltLine Townhomes Start Taking Shape
stories.summary: GW Parkside (formerly Hopkins and White Townhomes) is bringing 23 new for-sale units to the West End near the BeltLine. Developed by Ackerman & Co., the same folks behind Lee + White, the first seven units should hit the market this spring. It's one of the few new townhome projects along the southwest BeltLine corridor.
stories.body:
Ackerman & Co. is making moves along the BeltLine again. Their latest project, now called GW Parkside, is starting to take shape on Hopkins Street in the West End. The same developers who transformed Lee + White into the bustling office and food destination are now tackling residential with 23 new townhomes that should appeal to people wanting to live near the BeltLine without paying Old Fourth Ward prices.

What's interesting about this project is the timing. While everyone's been focused on the eastside BeltLine developments, the western segments have been slower to see this kind of residential infill. GW Parkside could be a signal that the West End is finally catching up to other BeltLine neighborhoods in terms of new construction and buyer interest.

The first seven units are supposed to hit the market this spring, and I'm curious to see how they're priced compared to similar new construction in Grant Park or Cabbagetown. If Ackerman can deliver quality townhomes at a reasonable price point, this could be exactly what the West End needs to continue its renaissance. Plus, being able to walk to Lee + White for coffee and lunch doesn't hurt the sales pitch.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering of GW Parkside townhomes in West End near BeltLine

stories.headline: Robert Montwaid's New Eastside Food Hall Opens in April
stories.summary: The creator of NYC's Gansevoort Market is bringing his third Atlanta food concept to the new Vela Park community near East Lake Golf Club. Vela Park Food Works will feature six chef-driven concepts with craft cocktails and outdoor dining when it opens mid-April. It's exactly the kind of amenity these new mixed-use developments need to feel like real neighborhoods.
stories.body:
This is pretty exciting news for the eastside food scene. Robert Montwaid, the guy who created New York's legendary Gansevoort Market, is opening his third Atlanta food hall at the new Vela Park development near East Lake Golf Club. Vela Park Food Works is set to debut in mid-April with six different chef-driven concepts, craft cocktails, and plenty of outdoor seating.

What makes this particularly interesting is the location. East Lake has been undergoing this quiet transformation with new apartments and townhomes, but it's been lacking the kind of dining and entertainment options that make a neighborhood feel complete. Having someone with Montwaid's track record come in and create a proper food destination could be exactly what this area needs to establish its own identity.

I'm also curious to see how this plays out compared to other food halls around town. The key will be whether they can attract chefs who bring something unique rather than just another collection of generic concepts. If Montwaid can replicate even a fraction of what made Gansevoort Market special, East Lake residents (and the rest of us) are going to have a new reason to venture over to that part of town.
stories.imageAlt: Interior rendering of Vela Park Food Works food hall

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Brings Asian Comfort Food to Inman Park
stories.summary: Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez just opened their first restaurant on Elizabeth Street, right off the Freedom Park Trail. Expect creative bao like soft shell crab and chicken longanisa, plus Peking chicken platters and cocktails like Thai tea martinis. The pop-up-turned-restaurant represents the kind of chef-driven spots that make Inman Park's food scene special.
stories.body:
There's something really exciting happening on Elizabeth Street in Inman Park. Mushi Ni, the new Asian-American comfort food spot from chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez, just opened its doors after building a following as a pop-up. Both Le and Jimenez have serious restaurant credentials (Jimenez worked for Gordon Ramsay in NYC), and you can taste that experience in every dish.

The menu is exactly what you'd want from a neighborhood spot: approachable but creative, with dishes that make you want to come back and try everything. The soft shell crab bao is already getting buzz, and their Peking chicken platter feeds two people for just $25, which feels like a steal in this market. They're also doing interesting cocktails like a Thai tea martini that actually works.

What I love about this opening is the location. Being right off the Freedom Park Trail means it's walkable for folks in Inman Park, Candler Park, and Little Five Points. Plus, having it in a neighborhood spot rather than some massive development gives it that intimate feel that makes you want to become a regular. If Le and Jimenez can maintain this level of quality, Mushi Ni could easily become one of those places you recommend to everyone.
stories.imageAlt: Peking chicken platter from Mushi Ni restaurant

stories.headline: Lewis Barbecue Brings Texas-Style BBQ to the BeltLine
stories.summary: The acclaimed Austin pitmaster John Lewis has opened his Atlanta outpost at Ansley Mall, complete with six massive smokers in a panoramic loft overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the BeltLine. The 24/7 smoking operation is turning out some of the best brisket in the city, bringing serious barbecue credentials to the ever-evolving BeltLine corridor.
stories.body:
John Lewis has brought something special to the BeltLine. The pitmaster behind one of Austin's most celebrated barbecue joints has set up shop at Ansley Mall with a setup unlike anything you've seen in Atlanta. Six 22-foot smokers fill a spacious loft with panoramic windows overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the BeltLine, running 24 hours a day in staggered shifts like some kind of beautiful, smoky factory.

Lewis helped weld those smokers himself from 1,000-gallon propane tanks, and the attention to detail shows in every bite. This isn't your typical Atlanta barbecue; it's proper Texas-style brisket with the kind of smoke ring and bark that barbecue nerds obsess over. The location at Ansley Mall might seem random, but being right on the BeltLine makes perfect sense for drawing crowds from across the city.

What's really impressive is how Lewis has managed to maintain Austin-level quality while scaling up for Atlanta's market. The brisket is legitimately some of the best you'll find in the city, and the whole operation feels both serious and accessible. For BeltLine walkers and cyclists, this is going to become a regular pit stop. Just don't expect to walk in and immediately get a table during peak hours.
stories.imageAlt: Loaded barbecue platter from Lewis Barbecue showing brisket and sides

stories.headline: Decatur's Complete Streets Makeover Nearly Finished
stories.summary: The West Howard Avenue project is wrapping up after nearly a decade of planning, transforming the corridor from Commerce Drive to Paden Circle into a calmer, more bike and pedestrian-friendly street. The new two-way cycle track with concrete barriers represents exactly the kind of infrastructure investment that makes neighborhoods more livable.
stories.body:
It's taken nearly a decade, but Decatur is finally wrapping up its ambitious Complete Streets project on West Howard Avenue. The transformation from high-speed car corridor to people-friendly street is pretty dramatic when you see it in person. We're talking new sidewalks, infrastructure upgrades, and most importantly, a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers that actually makes cycling feel safe.

The project runs from Commerce Drive near Decatur High School all the way to Paden Circle at the northern edge of East Lake MARTA station. That's about a mile of completely reimagined streetscape that prioritizes pedestrians and cyclists without eliminating car access. It's the kind of thoughtful infrastructure investment that cities talk about but don't always deliver.

What's particularly smart about this project is how it connects to existing bike infrastructure and transit. Having a protected bike lane that links downtown Decatur to the East Lake MARTA station creates real transportation options for people who don't want to drive everywhere. Plus, the traffic calming measures should make the whole corridor safer for everyone, including drivers. This is how you do Complete Streets right.
stories.imageAlt: New protected bike lanes on West Howard Avenue in Decatur

stories.headline: City Council Advances ICE Restrictions as Firefighter Contract Dispute Flares
stories.summary: The Public Safety Committee unanimously approved resolutions barring ICE detention facilities within city limits and requiring documentation of ICE activities during police operations. Meanwhile, dozens of residents criticized Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign the firefighters union contract that council ratified last year. Both issues head to the full council vote on April 20.
stories.body:
There was a lot of tension in the air at Monday's Public Safety Committee meeting. The committee unanimously advanced two resolutions aimed at controlling ICE activities in Atlanta, including one that would ban ICE detention facilities within city limits and another requiring APD to document when federal immigration agents are present during their operations. Both measures will go to the full council for a vote on April 20.

But what really had people fired up was the ongoing dispute over the firefighters union contract. Dozens of residents showed up to criticize Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign the contract that the city council already ratified back in April 2025. This is Atlanta's first-ever firefighter union contract, and the mayor's refusal to sign it has created this weird standoff where nobody seems sure what happens next.

The timing of both issues is pretty significant given everything that's happening at the federal level with immigration enforcement. Atlanta's trying to position itself as a city that won't cooperate with mass deportation efforts while also dealing with internal labor disputes that affect public safety. It's exactly the kind of complex political moment that tests whether local government can actually deliver on its promises to residents.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta City Council Public Safety Committee meeting with residents speaking

stories.headline: ICE Agents Deploy to Hartsfield-Jackson as Airport Chaos Continues
stories.summary: Armed federal immigration officers in tactical gear are now patrolling Atlanta's airport to help with security as TSA workers continue calling out during the partial government shutdown. The airport is recommending four-hour arrival times while nearly 3,500 TSA agents nationwide missed work Monday. It's a surreal scene that perfectly captures the dysfunction of the federal shutdown.
stories.body:
The scene at Hartsfield-Jackson on Monday was something else. Armed ICE agents in full tactical gear patrolling the terminals while travelers waited in security lines for hours. Mayor Dickens was quick to assure everyone that these agents won't be doing immigration enforcement, but honestly, the optics are pretty intense for anyone trying to catch a flight.

The whole situation stems from TSA workers continuing to call out during the partial government shutdown. Nearly 3,500 agents nationwide didn't show up for work on Monday, and Atlanta got hit particularly hard. The airport is now recommending that passengers arrive four hours early, which is just wild when you think about it. Four hours for a domestic flight feels more like international travel from the 1990s.

What's frustrating is how this affects everyone trying to get in and out of Atlanta. Business travelers, families visiting relatives, people with medical appointments, they're all caught up in this federal dysfunction. And having ICE agents filling in for TSA workers just adds another layer of weirdness to an already stressful situation. Hopefully this gets resolved soon because Atlanta's economy depends on that airport running smoothly.
stories.imageAlt: Federal immigration officers at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport

stories.headline: DeKalb Parents Protest School Closure Plans at Lakeside Meeting
stories.summary: Hundreds of frustrated parents packed Lakeside High School to voice opposition to DeKalb County Schools' revised closure list. Oak Grove and Evansdale families took to the streets before the meeting, demanding a student-first approach to the district's financial crisis. The protests highlight the deep community divisions over how to handle the district's budget problems.
stories.body:
The parking lot at Lakeside High School was pretty intense on Sunday night. Hundreds of parents showed up for another DeKalb County Schools closure meeting, with Oak Grove and Evansdale families staging a protest outside before the official session even started. Signs reading 'Save Small Schools' and 'Students First' made it clear that parents aren't buying the district's rationale for these closures.

What struck me about talking to these parents is how betrayed they feel by the whole process. Samuel Howe, an Oak Grove parent, called it 'a farce' and said the district isn't really listening to community input. That sentiment was pretty common among the protesters, who feel like the closures are a done deal regardless of what they say at these meetings.

The whole situation highlights the impossible position DeKalb Schools finds itself in. The district clearly has financial problems that need addressing, but closing neighborhood schools hits at the heart of what families value most about their communities. There's got to be a better way to balance the budget than forcing kids into longer commutes and breaking up school communities that have existed for decades. These parents deserve real solutions, not just more meetings.
stories.imageAlt: Parents and children protesting outside DeKalb County school meeting
